title: Burrow V1: OS-isolated sandbox runtime for coding agents
description: Implement Burrow V1 per SPEC.md — local-first, single-user CLI/library that spins up sandboxed workspaces (bwrap on Linux, sandbox-exec on macOS), runs CLI-based coding agents inside them, persists run state in SQLite, streams events, and provides steering via inbox messages. ~3-4k LOC, 12-16 days estimate, 10 implementation phases.

title: Wire bw prompt: dispatch a registered agent against a burrow
description:
bw init <agent> + bw agents add <id> let users declare runtimes in burrow.toml (Phase 8), but there's no CLI to actually start a run — the user can't sit at a shell and say 'run claude-code in this burrow with this prompt'. Today bw send queues an inbox message but nothing pulls it through to a sandboxed spawn.

Acceptance:
- bw prompt <id> '<message>' creates a run row, dispatches via the registered AgentRuntime, streams events as NDJSON (or pretty when TTY), and persists everything.
- Picks the burrow's default agent from burrow.toml [[agents]] (first row) when --agent is omitted; --agent <id> overrides.
- Honors SandboxProfile.setEnv (resolved by Phase 8 up wiring).
- Surfaces install-check failures with the existing AgentNotInstalled error (exit code per SPEC §16).
- Gates on burrow state (refuse on stopped/destroyed; suggest bw attach).

title: forward host ~/.claude credential cache (read-only) into claude-code burrows
description:
SPEC §17.4: "If the host has Claude Code authenticated (~/.claude/), Burrow detects this and forwards the credential cache (read-only) into burrows that declare claude-code as a runtime. No second login required."

Currently not wired. After the toolchainPaths fix (commit 3324540), `bw prompt` reaches claude successfully but the agent immediately reports "Not logged in · Please run /login" because ~/.claude is not visible inside the sandbox.

Scope (LOCAL BURROWS ONLY):
- Detect ~/.claude/ on the host (and optionally only when claude-code is registered for the burrow).
- Mount it read-only into the sandbox. Linux/bwrap: --ro-bind to $HOME/.claude (where HOME=/workspace inside bwrap). macOS/seatbelt: file-read* allow on the realpath, since HOME inside the sandbox is the workspace path on the host.
- Sandbox profile field: extend SandboxProfile with a dedicated `agentCredentials` slot, OR reuse readOnlyMounts. The former gives us a place to attach per-runtime metadata later (e.g. sapling/codex caches).
- Plumb through claude-code runtime's installCheck so up.ts knows whether the runtime wants the cache.
- Opt-out via burrow.toml: [agents.claude-code] forward_credentials = false.

Out of scope — deploy targets (fly/docker/tarball) deliberately do NOT forward ~/.claude:
- The OAuth token in ~/.claude/credentials.json is a single-user credential tied to a Claude Pro/Max plan; using it from an unattended remote machine is the wrong shape (rate limits, ToS, refresh lifecycle).
- Deployed burrows authenticate via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from [secrets], which claude-code already prefers over the OAuth cache when both are present. That path is wired by Phase 8's secret resolution and reaches the sandbox via SandboxProfile.setEnv.
- The Phase 9 work of delivering [secrets] across the dev-machine → target boundary (fly secrets set, docker --secret, tarball sidecar .env.example) is its own design problem and is filed separately under burrow-5ed6 or a dedicated child once Phase 9 starts.

Repro:
  bw destroy <id> && bw up && bw prompt <id> "ping"
  → "Not logged in · Please run /login"

title: fix: macOS restricted-network — regex-on-hostname rules never match (allow_domains is no-op)
description:
SPEC §17.4 credential forwarding fixed in burrow-980e — auth now works.
But `bw prompt` against a `network = "restricted"` burrow on darwin loops
on `api_retry` events because the destination filtering is fundamentally
not enforced. `network = "open"` works; `network = "restricted"` blocks
everything.

Root cause: src/provider/local/seatbelt.ts renderNetworkRules() emits
  (allow network-outbound (regex "^.*\\.api\\.anthropic\\.com$"))
  (allow network-outbound (regex "^api\\.anthropic\\.com$"))
sandbox-exec's network-outbound matches the destination address at
connect() time. After Bun/Node resolves DNS, the connection is made to
an IP (e.g. `1.2.3.4:443`), not the hostname. The hostname regex never
matches the IP form, so all outbound TCP is denied.

Empirical confirmation (host /tmp tests):
  - profile with current regex + DNS allowed → curl exit 7 (denied)
  - profile with `(remote tcp "*:443")` + DNS allowed → HTTP 405 (allowed)
  - profile with `(remote tcp)` (no DNS) → curl exit 6 (DNS still needed)
  - profile with regex `anthropic\.com` (no anchors) → still exit 7

Fix options:
  a) Resolve allowed_domains to IPs at `bw up` time and emit
     `(allow network-outbound (remote ip "1.2.3.4:443"))` rules. Brittle
     for CDN-fronted hosts whose IPs rotate (api.anthropic.com is fronted
     by CloudFront / similar; IPs change minute to minute).
  b) Allow `(remote tcp "*:443")` + `(remote tcp "*:80")` + DNS when
     network=restricted, with no hostname filtering at all. Honest about
     what we enforce on darwin (= deny only port-mapped local traffic);
     hostname allowlisting deferred to the userspace proxy.
  c) Ship the SPEC §25.3 userspace HTTP proxy now: bind a localhost
     proxy on the host, `(allow network-outbound (remote tcp "127.0.0.1:<port>"))`,
     proxy enforces the domain allowlist + DNS resolution. Same approach
     would land Linux restricted-network at the same time.

Until fixed, `network = "open"` is the only working policy on darwin.
SPEC §25.3 already flags userspace proxy as outstanding. Prefer (c).

Repro:
  bw up                       # burrow.toml has network = "restricted"
  bw prompt <id> "ping"       # 10 retries, error_status: null, error: "unknown"
  bw destroy <id> && bw up --network open && bw prompt <id> "ping"
                              # → "pong" in ~2s

title: bw up --network default 'none' overrode burrow.toml [sandbox].network
description: Commander.js .option(name, desc, default) writes the literal default into opts when the flag is omitted, so opts.network was always 'none'. collapseUpOptions forwarded it; resolveNetworkPolicy in up.ts saw flag !== undefined and ignored config.sandbox.network. Result: 'bw up' against a project whose burrow.toml declared network="restricted" still stored profile.network="none" in the DB, silently masking the burrow-14b6 proxy fix end-to-end. Discovered while verifying burrow-14b6 with a real bw prompt run. Fixed in 7336d0c by dropping the literal default — opts.network stays undefined when --network is omitted, and resolveNetworkPolicy falls through to burrow.toml correctly. Verified: bw prompt against network=restricted now returns 'pong' in ~2s.

title: burrow watch: TUI dashboard for live multi-burrow view
description:
Post-V1 first feature per SPEC §24. Multi-burrow live view, designed as the **pre-web-UI**: the load-bearing seam is a stable `DashboardSnapshot` view model (JSON shape) that today drives a TUI renderer and tomorrow drives a React frontend over `burrow serve` (§24). Same shape, two faces.

Architecture:
1. **View model** (`src/dashboard/types.ts`) — `DashboardSnapshot` + `BurrowCard` shapes. Stable, additive-only contract just like the §14.1 NDJSON event envelope. Public lib export.
2. **Snapshot builder** (pure) — `buildSnapshot(repos): DashboardSnapshot`. Aggregates burrows, current runs, inbox pending, last event ts.
3. **Snapshot stream** — `streamSnapshots(repos, bus, opts)`. Async generator driven by event bus + throttled rebuild. AbortSignal-aware.
4. **TUI renderer** (pure) — `renderSnapshot(snap, viewState, termSize): string`. Deterministic; golden-tested.
5. **TUI runtime** — alt-screen, raw mode, resize, keybinds (q/j/k/enter/esc). Detail pane per burrow with scrollable event tail.
6. **CLI command** — `burrow watch [--burrow ID...] [--interval MS] [--json]` (`--json` emits raw snapshots to stdout, the same shape `burrow serve` will WS-stream later).

Reference (architecture only, not impl): overstory/src/commands/dashboard.ts uses raw ANSI; we adopt that for zero-deps but layered behind the snapshot/render split it doesn't have.

title: claude-code in sandbox: Bash tool output file unreachable (/tmp/claude-501/...)
description:
Symptom
-------
Bash tool calls inside a burrowed claude-code spawn intermittently return:

    Exit code 1
    <bash output unavailable: output file /tmp/claude-501/-Users-jayminwest-Library-...
     could not be read (EPERM). This usually means another Claude Code process in the
     same project deleted it during startup cleanup.>

Key facts (from run_5gy69r13xz1n in bur_7tte9v6bcrc4)
-----------------------------------------------------
- Failures are concentrated on Bash; non-shell tools (Read, Glob, Grep, ToolSearch,
  TodoWrite, AskUserQuestion) all work fine — they don't touch /tmp/claude-501/.
- Failure pattern is intermittent: in the same run, `sd prime` (#6) and `sd show`
  (#8) succeeded while sibling `ml prime` (#4, #10, #12) failed. Repeatable retries
  also failed.
- The error message is Claude Code's own self-diagnosis — it explicitly blames
  'another Claude Code process in the same project deleted it during startup cleanup.'

Root cause
----------
Claude Code writes Bash command output to:
    ${TMPDIR-/tmp}/claude-${uid}/<sanitized-cwd>/<session-id>/tasks/<task-id>.output

The /tmp/claude-501/ root is **host-UID-keyed**, not session-keyed. Every new Claude
Code process performs a startup cleanup sweep across that root, and that sweep
races against any other Claude Code process (host-side OR another burrow) writing
output files. The burrow's claude session wrote the file, the host claude (or a
sibling burrow's claude) pruned it during cleanup, and read-back returned EPERM.

This is not actually a sandbox-profile problem (the seatbelt profile lets the
burrow read/write /tmp/claude-501 — otherwise the file wouldn't exist). It's a
shared-tmpdir collision that surfaces whenever burrow encourages running
multiple Claude Code instances on the same host (which is the whole point of
burrow).

Fix
---
Set TMPDIR per-burrow in the claude-code runtime spawn so each agent gets an
isolated /tmp/claude-501/ root inside its workspace:

  - Create <workspace>/.burrow-tmp/ in prepareWorkspace().
  - Set env TMPDIR=<workspace>/.burrow-tmp in buildSpawnCommand + buildResumeCommand.
  - The seatbelt profile already allows file-write* on the workspace subpath, so
    no profile change needed.
  - Add the dir to .gitignore for project burrows that materialize via worktree.

This isolates each burrowed claude from every other claude on the host —
including the one driving the user's terminal session.

Reproduction
------------
1. Have a host claude-code session running in /Users/jayminwest/Projects/os-eco/burrow.
2. `bw up && bw fork <project-id> --task ...`
3. `bw prompt <task-id> '<any prompt that triggers Bash tool calls>'`
4. Watch `bw logs <task-id> --follow` — Bash tool_results intermittently carry
   the EPERM message; non-Bash tools work fine.

Acceptance
----------
- Per-burrow TMPDIR isolation implemented in src/runtime/claude-code.ts (both
  spawn paths) and verified in claude-code.test.ts.
- A burrowed claude-code spawn run alongside a host claude-code session can
  execute 100 sequential Bash commands with zero EPERM failures.
- Linux (bwrap) parity verified.
- Mulch decision recorded under runtime explaining the why (so future runtimes
  with similar shared-tmp behavior get the same isolation by default).

Related
-------
- Discovered while running burrow-0a39 in bur_7tte9v6bcrc4 (run_5gy69r13xz1n).
- Mulch decision mx-... (a2c00ef) on --dangerously-skip-permissions is unrelated;
  this is a separate, deeper issue that surfaces only after permissions don't
  block.

title: sandbox: globally-installed bun packages (ml, sd, cn, ov, …) unreachable inside burrow
description:
Symptom
-------
Inside a burrow, executing any globally-installed bun-based CLI from os-eco
(ml, sd, cn, ov, sapling, greenhouse) fails with:

    error loading current directory
    error: An internal error occurred (CouldntReadCurrentDirectory)

Built-in shell builtins (echo, pwd) and sandbox-allowed binaries work fine —
only forks to bun-shebang scripts fail.

Repro
-----
1. `bw fork <project-id>`
2. `bw prompt <task-id> 'run: ml prime'`
3. Watch the bash tool_result — `ml prime` errors as above. `echo hello`
   in the same run succeeds.

Root cause
----------
Globally-installed bun packages live at:
    ~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/<scope>/<pkg>/

…with stub symlinks under `~/.bun/bin/<name>` that have a bun-shebang and
import the real script. The seatbelt + bwrap profiles mount only
`~/.bun/bin` (because that's where the `bun` toolchain binary lives), not
`~/.bun/install`. When a stub like `ml` runs, bun follows the symlink to
`~/.bun/install/global/node_modules/@os-eco/mulch-cli/src/cli.ts`, which is
in a sandbox read-denied path — so bun fails to load the script and emits
the (misleading) cwd error.

Verified by inspecting the binary layout:
    /Users/jayminwest/.bun/bin/ml -> ../install/global/node_modules/@os-eco/mulch-cli/src/cli.ts
    /Users/jayminwest/.bun/bin/sd -> ../install/global/node_modules/seeds/src/index.ts
    (every os-eco CLI in /Users/jayminwest/.bun/install/global/node_modules/ has the same shape)

`expandToolchainBinDirs` (src/toolchain/paths.ts) handles symlinked
*toolchain binaries* — it would mount the realpath dir if the toolchain
itself were a symlink — but it doesn't traverse symlinks of arbitrary
binaries that happen to live in the toolchain bin dir.

Impact
------
- Blocks dogfooding: the prompt template the user sends to burrowed agents
  literally starts with 'run ml prime'. Agents waste tokens looping on a
  command that can't execute.
- Affects every os-eco tool the user has installed globally.
- Generalizes to any user-installed-via-bun CLI (rsbuild, prettier, etc.)

Fix candidates
--------------
1. **Auto-mount bun's global install root.** When bun is in toolchainPaths,
   detect and add `~/.bun/install/global/node_modules` to readOnlyMounts.
   Cleanest; resolves once globally for all bun packages. The path is
   stable across bun versions (`bun pm bin -g` returns `~/.bun/bin`, parent
   is `~/.bun`, install root is `<parent>/install/global/node_modules`).
2. **Walk `~/.bun/bin` symlinks at `burrow up`.** For each entry that's a
   symlink, contribute its realpath dir. More principled (covers any
   non-standard install layouts) but slower and more code.
3. **User-config: `[sandbox] read_only_paths = [...]`.** Lets the user
   declare extra mounts in burrow.toml. Most flexible but pushes friction
   onto every adopter.

Recommend (1) for burrow's own dogfooding (zero-config), with (3) added in
parallel as the general escape hatch (other ecosystems will need it).

Acceptance
----------
- A burrowed claude-code spawn can run `ml prime`, `sd prime`, `cn list`
  successfully and parse their output.
- Equivalent fix lands for bwrap on Linux (~/.bun layout is identical).
- Seatbelt + bwrap profile tests assert the bun install root is mounted
  when bun is a declared toolchain.
- Unit test on `collectToolchainPaths` (or a new helper) covers the
  bun-install resolution.

Discovered while
----------------
Iterating on burrow-8452. The /tmp EPERM fix made shell builtins work but
exposed this second, separate gap.

title: design discussion: target isolation model for burrows on macOS vs Linux
description:
**Not a fix issue — a design conversation issue.** The intent is to walk through how the user wants burrow's isolation story to look, with another agent, then close this with a decision (or spawn implementation seeds from the outcome).

Context: where we are today
---------------------------
While debugging burrow-8452 we surfaced that the V1 sandbox guarantees on
macOS and Linux are not symmetric, and that the SPEC overstates what
macOS gives you.

**macOS (sandbox-exec / Seatbelt) — permission filter, not filesystem
isolation.** The burrowed agent runs on the real host filesystem. No
chroot, no mount namespace, no overlay. The workspace at
\`~/Library/Application Support/burrow/.../bur_xxx\` is a real directory
the user can \`cd\` into. \`/tmp\`, \`/Users\`, \`/dev\` are the host's. The
profile is a deny-default allow-list applied at syscall time. Today's
allow-list (post-burrow-8452):
  - workspace: read+write
  - /usr, /System, /Library, /bin, /sbin, /private/etc, /private/var/db,
    /private/var/select, /dev: read-only
  - /private/tmp, /private/var/folders: read+write (added in 8d9c7bc)
  - /dev/null, /dev/dtracehelper: write
  - toolchain bin dirs: read-only
  - readOnlyMounts (e.g. ~/.claude, ~/.claude.json): read-only
  - SSH agent socket: read+write
  - network: restricted via host-side userspace proxy
What the agent **can** still do that may surprise users: write to the
host's /tmp (collisions with the user's own claude-code session are real,
not theoretical — that's why the original /tmp/claude-501 EPERM was
shaped the way it was), read the host's per-user temp at /private/var/folders,
read forwarded ~/.claude credentials, see real toolchain binaries on the
host PATH.

**Linux (bwrap) — genuine namespace isolation.** Mount namespace, PID
namespace, network namespace. Workspace bind-mounted at /workspace.
\`/tmp\` is a private tmpfs (or whatever's bound), not the host's /tmp.
Paths the user didn't mount don't exist inside the burrow.

**Spec drift.** SPEC §8.5 says the sandbox 'cannot read or write outside
the workspace.' That was true of the original profile. Allow-rules have
accumulated as real workloads needed them (toolchains, /tmp writes,
/var/folders, /dev/null, now /tmp reads). Each addition is individually
defensible; the cumulative effect widens the trust boundary in ways
SPEC doesn't currently describe.

**Live wart.** claude-code hardcodes /tmp for its bash-output cache and
ignores \$TMPDIR (verified: the TMPDIR redirect added in af21642 has no
effect on the actual file path claude writes to). So per-burrow temp
isolation via env var alone won't work for claude-code; it requires
either a sandbox-level virtual /tmp or upstream changes.

Questions to explore with the user
----------------------------------
1. Target isolation model on macOS — permission-fence vs synthesize vs
   defer to V2 / Linux-only.
2. Should /tmp be the host's /tmp at all on macOS?
3. What's the right behavior when an agent hardcodes a host-shared
   path (claude-code → /tmp/claude-\${uid}/)?
4. Strict vs ergonomic mode in burrow.toml.
5. Where does burrow-aa46 (bun install root mount) fit?
6. SPEC §8.4/§8.5 documentation pass.

(Original question detail in pre-resolution version; preserved in git
history of .seeds/.)

==================================================================
Resolution (2026-05-07)
==================================================================

Discussion with the user resolved all six questions. Decisions:

| Q  | Decision |
|----|----------|
| Q1 | **Linux is canonical, macOS is best-effort + thin permission filter.** End goal is swarms of agents in cloud-deployed Linux containers, so Linux gets first-class isolation guarantees. macOS stays as developer-ergonomics mode — useful, not a security boundary. Synthesizing bwrap parity on macOS via DYLD/wrapper tricks is a tar pit and rejected. |
| Q2 | **Principle: no host /tmp. Reality: macOS keeps /tmp r+w in V1.** Blocked on (a) claude-code hardcoding /tmp and ignoring \$TMPDIR (upstream issue), and (b) sandbox-exec having no bind-mount primitive (so we can't redirect /tmp at the path level). Linux already private-tmpfs's /tmp via bwrap. Revisit when (a) lands. |
| Q3 | **Best-effort accommodation for agents we ship; upstream the underlying bugs; user-spawned binaries accept collision and document.** For claude-code/sapling we have leverage and should fix upstream. For arbitrary user binaries that hardcode host-shared paths, document the collision risk and move on. |
| Q4 | **Ergonomic profile only in V1; no \`strict\` knob.** Since macOS isn't framed as a security boundary anyway, splitting the profile adds complexity without buying real isolation. |
| Q5 | **No change.** burrow-aa46 (bun install root mount) and the in-flight burrow-a1b1 (generic toolchain symlink walk + \`[sandbox] read_only_paths\` escape hatch) both fit fine under ergonomic permission-fence on macOS. They're widening the allow-list pragmatically, which the Q4 decision endorses. |
| Q6 | **Defer.** SPEC is becoming outdated faster than it's worth rewriting. Migration to ROADMAP.md is planned separately; isolation-model documentation rolls into that. |

Bwrap-in-container spike (informs deployment direction)
-------------------------------------------------------
Verified empirically (Ubuntu 24.04 host, Docker 28.4):
- Vanilla \`docker run\` blocks bwrap — Ubuntu 24.04 ships
  \`kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=1\`, and the
  docker-default AppArmor profile inherits the block.
- Minimum non-privileged invocation that works:

      docker run \\
        --security-opt apparmor=unconfined \\
        --security-opt seccomp=unconfined \\
        --security-opt systempaths=unconfined \\
        --cap-add SYS_ADMIN \\
        <image>

- \`--privileged\` also works as a fallback but relaxes the outer
  container far more than necessary.
- Doesn't change any decision above. Confirms the dev-container path
  is viable with a known recipe and informs deployment posture.

Spawned child seeds
-------------------
- **burrow-1c19** — devcontainer: Linux-canonical local dev environment
  (lets macOS users opt into real isolation locally; same artifact
  shape as eventual deploy)
- **burrow-fbdf** — upstream: claude-code Bash output cache should
  respect \$TMPDIR (unblocks Q2 macOS /tmp deny when fixed)
- **burrow-7ba7** — deploy posture: prefer burrow-on-host over
  burrow-in-pod for production swarms (userns nesting nuance)

Related
-------
- burrow-8452 (closed; seatbelt /tmp + /dev/null fixes)
- burrow-aa46 (closed; bun install root mount)
- burrow-a1b1 (in_progress; generic toolchain symlink walk + read_only_paths)
closeReason: Resolved via design discussion 2026-05-07. Decisions documented in seed body; spawned burrow-1c19 (devcontainer), burrow-fbdf (upstream claude-code TMPDIR), burrow-7ba7 (deploy posture). Headline: Linux is canonical, macOS is best-effort + thin permission filter; ergonomic profile only in V1.

title: sandbox: generic toolchain bin-dir symlink walk + [sandbox] read_only_paths escape hatch
description:
Follow-up to burrow-aa46. The fix shipped there (auto-mount
~/.bun/install/global/node_modules when bun is a declared toolchain) is
bun-specific. uv-tool-install, pyenv shims, nvm, rustup, mise/asdf all
have the same shape — `bin/<stub>` symlink → `install/<real>` — and would
hit the same `error loading current directory` (or equivalent) inside a
burrow. Cargo is mostly safe because `~/.cargo/bin/*` holds real binaries,
not symlinks.

Plan
----
Two parts, both wanted:

1. **Generic symlink walk over toolchain bin dirs.** At `burrow up`, for
   each toolchain bin dir we already mount, enumerate its entries and
   follow symlinks transitively. Contribute `dirname(realpath)` for each
   to `profile.toolchainPaths`, deduped. This catches uv/pyenv/nvm/rustup
   automatically without per-tool knowledge. Cost: ~100 stat calls per
   bin dir at `burrow up` (fine — `up` is not high-frequency). No new
   bwrap/seatbelt code; the existing toolchainPaths plumbing renders it.

2. **`[sandbox] read_only_paths` in burrow.toml.** Explicit escape hatch
   for anything (1) misses — tools that read config from `$HOME`, data
   files outside the bin-symlink chain, etc. Schema: array of host
   absolute paths. Lands as `profile.readOnlyMounts` entries.

Once (1) lands the bun-specific helper from burrow-aa46
(`resolveBunGlobalInstallDir` + the `binary === "bun"` branch in
`collectToolchainPaths`) becomes dead weight and should be removed in
the same PR.

Tradeoffs / open questions
--------------------------
- (1) may mount more directories than strictly needed. That's the
  honest cost of the implicit mechanism. Acceptable in exchange for
  zero-config dogfooding across the bun/uv/pyenv ecosystem.
- Should (1) bound how far it walks? E.g. cap at 2 hops, or refuse
  realpath targets outside `$HOME`. Probably yes — defends against a
  rogue symlink to `/etc` etc.
- Should (1) only walk bin dirs that came from declared toolchains, or
  ALL bin dirs? Declared-only is safer.
- (2) needs schema validation (paths must be absolute, must exist at
  `up` time?), with clear error from the doctor.

Acceptance
----------
- Bun-specific code path from burrow-aa46 deleted; the generic walk
  covers `ml`, `sd`, `cn`, `ov`, `sapling`, `greenhouse` from inside a
  burrow.
- A test profile that declares a toolchain whose bin dir contains an
  arbitrary symlink → that symlink's realpath dir lands on
  `profile.toolchainPaths`.
- `[sandbox] read_only_paths = ["~/foo"]` lands on
  `profile.readOnlyMounts`; tilde and `$HOME` expansion documented.
- Doctor surfaces non-existent `read_only_paths` entries with a
  clean diagnostic (don't silently drop).

Discovered while
----------------
Reviewing the burrow-aa46 fix. The original seed even called this out:
"Recommend (1) for burrow's own dogfooding (zero-config), with (3)
added in parallel as the general escape hatch."

title: devcontainer: Linux-canonical local dev environment for burrow
description:
Spawned from burrow-0fab (macOS vs Linux isolation design discussion).

Motivation
----------
0fab decided: Linux is burrow's canonical isolation target; macOS is
best-effort + thin permission filter. To make that real for developers
on macOS, we need a Linux dev container they can run burrow inside —
same bwrap, same toolchain, basically the same artifact we'll
eventually deploy.

Recipe (verified via spike on Ubuntu 24.04 host, Docker 28.4)
-------------------------------------------------------------
Vanilla Docker run does NOT work — Ubuntu 24.04 ships
\`kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=1\` by default, which
blocks bwrap's \`unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER)\` from any container without an
explicit AppArmor profile. Minimum non-privileged invocation:

    docker run \\
      --security-opt apparmor=unconfined \\
      --security-opt seccomp=unconfined \\
      --security-opt systempaths=unconfined \\
      --cap-add SYS_ADMIN \\
      <image>

\`apparmor=unconfined\` lifts the userns block. \`SYS_ADMIN\` lets bwrap
bring up loopback in the new netns (otherwise \`Failed RTM_NEWADDR\`).
\`systempaths=unconfined\` lets it mount /proc past Docker's masked paths
(otherwise \`Can't mount proc on /newroot/proc\`). Without all four, bwrap
either can't create namespaces or can't finish setup. \`--privileged\`
also works as a fallback but relaxes the outer container far more than
necessary.

What ships
----------
- Dockerfile (Ubuntu 24.04 base, bun, bwrap, git, build essentials)
- compose.yml or .devcontainer/devcontainer.json with the 4 security flags
- Bind-mount of project root → /workspace
- README section: how to enter the dev container, why the 4 flags

Acceptance
----------
- \`docker compose up && docker compose exec dev bash\` lands in a shell
  where \`bun test && bun run lint && bun run typecheck\` all pass and
  \`bw up && bw fork ...\` actually executes (i.e., bwrap works inside
  the dev container).
- The 4-flag recipe is documented inline so the next person doesn't
  have to re-spike.

Related
-------
- burrow-0fab (parent decision)
- Eventually: deploy posture seed (sibling — burrow-on-host vs
  burrow-in-pod nuance leans on the same userns finding)

title: upstream: claude-code Bash output cache should respect $TMPDIR
description:
Spawned from burrow-0fab (macOS vs Linux isolation design discussion).

Context
-------
claude-code writes Bash tool output to:
    \${TMPDIR-/tmp}/claude-\${uid}/<sanitized-cwd>/<session>/tasks/<task-id>.output

But the path it actually opens is hardcoded to \`/tmp\`, even when
\$TMPDIR is set. Verified during burrow-8452: commit af21642 set
\`TMPDIR=<workspace>/.burrow-tmp\` for every claude-code spawn inside
a burrow, and the file still landed in \`/tmp/claude-\${uid}/...\`.

Why this blocks burrow on macOS
-------------------------------
0fab's Q2 decision: in principle burrow should not allow host-shared
\`/tmp\` writes on macOS. We can't act on that in V1 because (a)
sandbox-exec has no bind-mount primitive (so we can't redirect /tmp at
the path level), and (b) blocking host /tmp r+w wholesale breaks
claude-code (and other tools that hardcode /tmp). If claude-code
honored \$TMPDIR for the output cache, burrow could deny host /tmp on
macOS and per-burrow temp isolation would actually work end-to-end.

What to do
----------
1. File an issue with anthropics/claude-code linking to:
   - The burrow-8452 reproduction
   - The hardcoded /tmp behavior (verified empirically; haven't located
     the exact source line — anyone picking this up should grep
     claude-code's open source bits or include a minimal repro)
2. Track the upstream issue in this seed.
3. When merged: revisit 0fab Q2 — flip macOS profile to deny host /tmp.

Related
-------
- burrow-8452 (closed; per-burrow TMPDIR + seatbelt /tmp r+w as the V1 workaround)
- burrow-0fab (parent decision)

title: deploy posture: prefer burrow-on-host over burrow-in-pod (userns nesting)
description:
Spawned from burrow-0fab (macOS vs Linux isolation design discussion).

The decision
------------
For deploying burrow swarms in the cloud, prefer:
- burrow daemon running on a Linux host (VM, Fly Machine, EC2) — no
  outer container

over:
- burrow daemon running inside a managed pod (Kubernetes, ECS Fargate,
  Cloud Run)

Why
---
bwrap needs unprivileged user namespaces. On modern Linux hosts that
works directly. Inside a managed container, the outer runtime's
default security profile typically blocks userns creation:
- Ubuntu 24.04 hosts ship \`kernel.apparmor_restrict_unprivileged_userns=1\`.
  Containers without an explicit AppArmor profile can't \`unshare(CLONE_NEWUSER)\`.
- Docker default seccomp is fine, but the docker-default AppArmor profile
  blocks userns; you need \`--security-opt apparmor=unconfined\`.
- bwrap also wants \`SYS_ADMIN\` (loopback in new netns) and
  \`systempaths=unconfined\` (mount /proc past masked paths).

Empirically (burrow-0fab spike, Ubuntu 24.04 host, Docker 28.4): the
minimum viable in-container invocation is 4 security overrides:

    --security-opt apparmor=unconfined
    --security-opt seccomp=unconfined
    --security-opt systempaths=unconfined
    --cap-add SYS_ADMIN

In production K8s / ECS / Cloud Run terms that's a privileged-workload
waiver in most clusters' admission policy. Possible but expensive to
negotiate, and the security relaxation of the outer container partly
defeats the point of nesting.

When burrow-in-pod is fine
--------------------------
- Self-managed K8s where you control admission policy — write the
  pod-security-policy or PSA exception once, done.
- Single-tenant clusters with no shared-trust constraints.
- Dev/CI environments where the outer-container relaxation is
  acceptable.

When burrow-on-host is the right call
-------------------------------------
- Multi-tenant managed K8s/ECS/Cloud Run.
- Anywhere admission policy is restrictive and not yours to change.
- Default for production swarms — fewer moving parts, full bwrap
  isolation, easier to audit.

What to do
----------
- When SPEC migrates to ROADMAP, include this in the deployment
  section.
- If/when greenhouse or overstory grow first-class deploy guides for
  burrow swarms, link this seed.
- Not actionable as code right now — informational decision record.

Related
-------
- burrow-0fab (parent decision)
- devcontainer seed (sibling — same userns finding, applied to local dev)

title: events: normalize claude-code init/rate-limit payloads into structured kinds
description:
During a dogfooding session against bur_2ydvj37tgjg7 the NDJSON event stream surfaced two non-text payloads under generic kinds:

- seq=2 `kind: text`, stream=stdout, payload `{type: rate_limit_event, rate_limit_info: {...}}` — claude-code rate-limit telemetry leaking through as a 'text' event.
- seq=3 `kind: thinking`, stream=stdout, payload `{text: ''}` — empty thinking event (no body) emitted before the first tool call.

Consumers of `burrow events --follow` and `watch --json` have to special-case these. Suggested fix: route `rate_limit_event` to its own kind (e.g. `telemetry` or `rate_limit`) and drop / coalesce empty-text `thinking` events at the runtime adapter (src/runtime/claude-code/*).

Repro: `bun src/cli/main.ts prompt <id> '...' --json` and grep the stream.

title: watch: large system/init payload dominates eventTail[0] in DashboardSnapshot
description:
The first event per run from claude-code is a `state_change` / `system/init` payload that includes the full tool list, slash commands, skills list, and memory paths. In `burrow watch --json` (and the TUI) this lands as `eventTail[0]` for every burrow. `--event-tail-cap` only trims from the right, so the heavy first event always ships.

Impact: a dashboard with N active burrows pays an O(N · ~3KB) snapshot floor, even when nothing else has happened. Noticeable when streaming snapshots over a coalescing window.

Options to consider:
1. Strip / summarize `system/init` payloads in the dashboard projection (keep kind+ts, drop tools/skills lists).
2. Treat `system/init` as metadata stored on the run row, not as event #1.
3. `--event-tail-cap` honored from the head as well, or a separate `--event-tail-skip-init` flag.

Repro: spawn a fresh task burrow, dispatch one prompt, run `burrow watch --once --json` and inspect `burrows[].eventTail[0].payload`.

title: HTTP API server (burrow serve)
description: Loopback-first HTTP surface mirroring the Client namespaces (burrows/runs/inbox/events/agent). Unblocks the warren control plane and any future external orchestrators (greenhouse, custom UIs). Transport: unix socket + localhost TCP (gated). Auth: bearer token from env. Event streaming as NDJSON over chunked HTTP, matching the existing tail wire shape. Adds a 'burrow serve' subcommand.

title: devcontainer: install ml + sd CLIs in image
description:
**Symptom**
Fresh agent attaching to the dev container hits `command not found` for both `ml` and `sd` despite CLAUDE.md mandating `ml prime` and `sd prime` at session start (and `sd close` / `sd sync` at session end).

**Repro**
1. `docker compose -f .devcontainer/compose.yml up -d`
2. `docker compose -f .devcontainer/compose.yml exec dev bash`
3. `ml --version` → `command not found`
4. `sd --version` → `command not found`

**Cause**
`.devcontainer/Dockerfile` only `bun install -g`s `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`. The CLAUDE.md "Project Expertise (Mulch)" and "Issue Tracking (Seeds)" sections both require these CLIs but neither package is baked into the image.

**Fix sketch**
Add to `.devcontainer/Dockerfile`, next to the existing claude-code install:

    RUN bun install -g @os-eco/mulch-cli @os-eco/seeds-cli

Pin to the versions CLAUDE.md references (mulch v0.8.0, seeds v0.4.0) if reproducible images are desired.

**Workaround until fixed**
`bun install -g @os-eco/mulch-cli @os-eco/seeds-cli` inside the running container.

**Surfaced by**
First-agent dogfood run from within the dev container, 2026-05-08.

title: sd plan submit: child seeds' transitive 'blocks' chain not mirrored in 'blockedBy'
description:
**Closed in favor of seeds-4a54** (in ../seeds/.seeds/) — the underlying bug is confirmed but the original symptom description here was incorrect.

**What this issue got right:** there IS a seeds bug in `sd plan submit` around blocks/blockedBy wiring.

**What was wrong:**
- The 37 doctor mismatches are from older plans (pl-a253: 21, pl-2085: 16) that were created before blockedBy population was complete. They are NOT caused by pl-5b40.
- The cited example 'burrow-2de5.blocks = [burrow-ee3c, burrow-1d64]' does not match the actual stored data — burrow-2de5.blocks is just [burrow-1d64]. pl-5b40 is bidirectionally consistent, which is why doctor doesn't flag it.

**The real bug** (filed as seeds-4a54): `sd plan submit` interprets step.blocks with the OPPOSITE semantic from PLAN_SPEC.md:248-257. LLMs writing plans per spec end up with the dependency chain inverted — `sd ready` in this repo currently surfaces burrow-fa21 (Phase 7 of pl-5b40) as ready instead of burrow-2de5 (Phase 0).

See seeds-4a54 for full trace and fix direction.
closeReason: Superseded by seeds-4a54 — original symptom description was inaccurate; real bug is plan submit inverting step.blocks direction (spec/impl mismatch).

title: ml doctor: stale dir_anchors in seeds[mx-20564e] reference .canopy / .overstory
description:
**Symptom**
`ml doctor` exits with one warning:

    ! 3 broken file anchor(s) found
        seeds[mx-20564e]: dir_anchors[] path not found: .canopy
        seeds[mx-20564e]: dir_anchors[] path not found: .overstory
        seeds[mx-20564e]: dir_anchors[] path not found: .overstory/agent-defs

Those paths belong to sister projects (canopy, overstory) and don't exist in the burrow checkout. The record itself is otherwise fine.

**Fix options**
- `ml doctor --fix` should drop missing dir_anchors automatically (per CLAUDE.md, that's the documented escape hatch).
- Or edit mx-20564e to keep only burrow-relative anchors.

Filing as a low-priority cleanup so a future agent can run `ml doctor --fix` (or trim the record by hand) without it getting lost in `ml doctor` noise.

**Surfaced by**
First-agent dogfood run, 2026-05-08. mulch v0.8.0.

title: devcontainer: postCreate doesn't 'bun link' so bw/burrow not on PATH
description:
**Symptom**
After a fresh `docker compose -f .devcontainer/compose.yml up -d`, `bw` and `burrow` are both `command not found`. The CLI is the project's own product; agents can't dogfood it without first running `bun link` manually.

**Cause**
`.devcontainer/devcontainer.json`'s `postCreateCommand` is just `bun install`. Nothing registers `package.json`'s `bin` entries (`burrow`, `bw` → `./src/cli/main.ts`).

**Fix sketch**
Change devcontainer.json's postCreateCommand to chain `bun link`:

    "postCreateCommand": "bun install && bun link"

That makes both `burrow` and `bw` available globally inside the container immediately after build (verified by hand this run — `bun link` then `bun link @os-eco/burrow-cli` got both binaries on PATH).

**Surfaced by**
First-agent dogfood run, 2026-05-08. Related: burrow-41da (same class of gap for ml/sd).

title: devcontainer: image missing node >=20, breaks 'bw doctor' and 'bw up'
description:
**Symptom**
`bw doctor` fails inside the dev container:

    ✗ toolchain.node >=20: not on PATH — install node >=20

`bw up` short-circuits with the same error before creating a burrow:

    [validation_error] burrow doctor failed:
      toolchain.node >=20: not on PATH — install node >=20

The dev container ships only Bun. `burrow.toml` declares `[toolchain] node = ">=20"`, so the project's own bootstrap path is unsatisfiable in the canonical Linux dev environment.

**Repro**
1. `docker compose -f .devcontainer/compose.yml up -d`
2. `docker compose ... exec dev bash`
3. `bun link && bun link @os-eco/burrow-cli` (workaround for burrow-NNN — bw not on PATH)
4. `bw doctor` → fails
5. `bw up` → blocked

**Fix sketch**
Add Node 20+ to `.devcontainer/Dockerfile`. Either NodeSource apt repo or fnm; pinning a single LTS would match the bun pin's spirit.

Or, if Node is genuinely not needed for burrow's own dev loop and the toolchain entry is aspirational, soften `burrow.toml` (drop the `node` requirement, or guard it behind a profile).

**Surfaced by**
First-agent dogfood run, 2026-05-08. Sibling to burrow-41da and the bun-link gap.

title: HTTP API: generate OpenAPI 3.1 spec from route definitions
description: Follow-up to pl-5b40 / burrow-1d64. Once the HTTP API is implemented, generate an OpenAPI 3.1 spec from the route definitions so external consumers (warren UI, custom dashboards, third-party tooling) get typed clients via codegen instead of hand-rolling against the wire format. Approach: derive the spec from the same Zod schemas used to validate request bodies (e.g. zod-to-openapi). Serve at GET /openapi.json behind the same auth as everything else, plus an unauthenticated GET /openapi.html that renders Scalar/Stoplight/Swagger UI for browsing. Lock-test the spec output via golden file (mx-1785cc pattern) so any breaking change is deliberate. Out of scope for the parent plan because (a) it presumes the route shapes are stable, (b) it's a polish layer that doesn't gate warren, (c) the same approach should land in warren's own HTTP API once it ships, so cross-tool consistency is worth its own seed.

title: devcontainer: forward host gpg + signing key so 'sd sync' / git commit work
description:
**Symptom**
Inside the dev container, `sd sync` (and any `git commit`) fails:

    Error: git commit failed: error: cannot run gpg: No such file or directory
    error: gpg failed to sign the data:
    (no gpg output)
    fatal: failed to write commit object

The host's global git config has `commit.gpgsign = true`, which is honored by the bind-mounted `/workspace/.git/config` chain. The container ships neither `gnupg` nor the user's signing key, so every commit attempt aborts before writing.

**Repro**
1. `docker compose -f .devcontainer/compose.yml up -d`
2. `docker compose ... exec dev bash`
3. Make any change in /workspace.
4. `sd sync` → fails as above. `git commit -m foo` fails the same way.

**Fix sketch (preferred — preserve signed commits inside container)**
- `.devcontainer/Dockerfile`: `apt-get install -y gnupg`.
- `.devcontainer/compose.yml`: bind-mount `~/.gnupg` (and ideally the gpg-agent socket dir under `${XDG_RUNTIME_DIR}`) into the container, read-only or read-write per host policy.
- Confirm `gpg --list-secret-keys` works as the `dev` user inside the container before committing.

**Workarounds while broken**
- `git -c commit.gpgsign=false commit ...` for individual commits.
- `git config --local commit.gpgsign false` inside the container (only touches /workspace/.git/config) — but `sd sync` doesn't honor a per-invocation flag, so the local override is the practical workaround for now.

**Surfaced by**
First-agent dogfood run, 2026-05-08. Sibling to burrow-41da / burrow-7600 / burrow-4fa1 — the dev container doesn't satisfy the host workflows the project's own protocols (CLAUDE.md session-close: `sd sync && git push`) require.

title: burrow serve: /watch query-param inconsistencies (once vs follow, no 1/0 alias)
description:
Smoke-testing `burrow serve` against the running daemon (port 4444) surfaced two related inconsistencies on `GET /watch`. Both stem from the same root: `/watch` uses `parseBoolean` on a `once` query param, while every other streaming surface (`/burrows/:id/events`, `/runs/:id/stream`) uses `parseStreamFollow` on a `follow` param.

## Finding 1 — `/watch?once=1` rejected; `/events?follow=1` accepted

- `GET /watch?once=true` → 200 (works)
- `GET /watch?once=1`    → 400 `"once must be 'true' or 'false'; got '1'"`
- `GET /burrows/:id/events?follow=1` → 200 (works)
- `GET /burrows/:id/events?follow=0` → 200 (works, drains)

The doc comment in `src/server/handlers.ts:511` literally says ``?once=1` collapses the stream'' — code and docs disagree. The intent stated at `src/server/handlers.ts:140-141` ("Streaming routes accept follow as 'true'/'false' OR '1'/'0' so curl users can write `?follow=1` (CLI muscle memory) without surprises") never made it to `/watch`.

## Finding 2 — `/watch?follow=0` silently ignored

- `GET /watch?follow=0` → 200, stream runs forever (curl times out; verified 39KB received in 4s before --max-time fired).

No validation error, no effect. Anyone coming from `/events` (where `?follow=0` is the documented drain-and-close switch) will hit this — I did during smoke testing. The streaming-param surface should be uniform across endpoints.

## Suggested fix

Either:
1. Replace `parseBoolean(... "once")` in `watchHandler` (handlers.ts:515) with `parseStreamFollow`, and rename the param from `once` to `follow` (with an inverted boolean) so the streaming surface is uniform.
2. Or: keep `once` semantics but accept `1`/`0` via `parseStreamFollow`, AND add a `follow` alias that maps to `!once` so users from `/events` aren't surprised.

Option 1 is the cleaner match for the rest of the surface; option 2 is the lower-risk shim if the wire shape is already fixed by external consumers.

Either way, also reject unrecognized query params on streaming endpoints — silent ignores cause stuck connections.

## Repro

```
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $BURROW_API_TOKEN" "http://127.0.0.1:4444/watch?once=1"
# → 400

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $BURROW_API_TOKEN" "http://127.0.0.1:4444/watch?follow=0"
# → hangs (silently ignored)
```

title: POST /burrows returns 501 — implement provisioning to unblock warren
description:
Burrow's OpenAPI 3.1 spec advertises POST /burrows but the handler returns 501 Not Implemented. Warren's entire provisioning flow (POST /burrows → seed mulch+canopy → POST /burrows/:id/runs) blocks on this endpoint shipping. Warren cannot be built against burrow until this lands.

Scope:
- Implement the provisioning handler (workspace bind, sandbox bringup) — replace the 501 stub
- Update OpenAPI in src/server/openapi/spec.ts to remove the 501 response and document the success contract
- Regenerate src/server/openapi/__golden__/openapi.json
- Add handler tests covering the success path and validation errors
- Wire through HttpClient (src/lib/http-client.ts) to mirror the in-process Client surface

Consumer: warren (warren/SPEC.md §4.3 step 2 has a footnote pointing to this work).

title: Define + implement run cancellation endpoints (POST /runs/:id/cancel + DELETE /runs/:id)
description:
Warren's runtime needs the ability to cancel an in-flight burrow run. Two endpoints with distinct semantics:

- POST /runs/:id/cancel — graceful cancellation. Optional body { reason: string }. Emits a cancel event on the stream. Run terminates with status=cancelled. Idempotent on already-completed runs (returns current state, not 4xx).
- DELETE /runs/:id — separate use case (TBD during implementation): hard-stop / force-kill an in-flight run, OR record removal post-completion. Decide which during implementation; document the choice in SPEC §27 or via OpenAPI description fields.

Both endpoints must be added to src/server/openapi/spec.ts; regenerate the golden file. Wire through HttpClient (src/lib/http-client.ts) to mirror the in-process Client surface. Add handler tests for graceful + idempotent + already-completed cases.

Consumer: warren (warren/SPEC.md §11 item 5 cancel-semantics open question is blocked on this).

title: Document /openapi.json + /openapi.html self-description in SPEC §27
description:
SPEC §27 did not mention the OpenAPI self-description endpoints. A patch landing in this same session adds a 'Self-description' sub-paragraph to §27 noting:
- GET /openapi.json (bearer-auth required) returns the full OpenAPI 3.1 spec
- GET /openapi.html (auth-exempt, loopback) renders the Scalar UI
- src/server/openapi/spec.ts is the hand-authored source; __golden__/openapi.json locks it via spec.test.ts

If the patch is reviewed and committed, this seed can close with the commit hash. Filed for audit trail.
